explanations.explanation: TCP is the reliable one. It is like sending a certified letter — you get confirmation it arrived. UDP is like throwing a note out the window and hoping someone catches it. Streaming video? UDP. SSH into a server? TCP. Every single byte needs to arrive.

Imagine your office building. Everyone is on the same floor physically, but you want the finance team and the engineering team to not be able to talk to each other's computers. VLANs let you create virtual walls.

Your home has one public IP address from your ISP. Every device in your house has a private IP. NAT is what your router does — it tracks which internal device made which request. Like a company switchboard: one public number, internal extensions.

A router just makes decisions. Every packet arrives, the router looks at the destination IP and asks: do I know how to get there? It checks its routing table. If it knows, it sends it. If no match at all, the packet dies.
